game_name: Wizardry: Proving Grounds of the Mad Overlord

design_philosophy.core_loop: Create party → Descend dungeon levels → Encounter monsters → Gain experience and treasure → Face final boss Werdna → Retrieve amulet
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Permadeath risk vs. progression investment; exploration discovery vs. becoming lost in maze; resource scarcity (healing items, spell uses) vs. dungeon depth
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Lead a custom-built adventuring party through a classic dungeon crawl, discovering treasure and facing escalating challenges, ultimately defeating an arch-wizard
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Uncompromising difficulty philosophy: no mid-dungeon saves, total party kill = game over (though bodies/items recoverable), mandatory manual mapmaking with graph paper, teleportation requires precise coordinate entry with risk of landing in traps. Establishes 'dungeon crawl' as a genre-defining playstyle. Party-based tactical system with class synergies (elite classes unlock through progression: Bishop, Samurai, Lord, Ninja). Fundamental design choice: mastery requires patience and planning, not reflexes.

difficulty.lives_system: Party-based: total party kill ends the run; however, new party can recover dead adventurers' bodies and items from dungeon
difficulty.continues_system: No continues within dungeon. Must exit dungeon to save progress. Japanese version had Turbo File device support for save states; US version removed this feature.
difficulty.checkpointing: No checkpoints within dungeon. Exit-to-save only. Later Wizardry games made this easier by restarting at death location; this original enforces full backtrack.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: party composition (race/alignment/class choices), four elite classes unlock through leveling (Bishop, Samurai, Lord, Ninja), spell selection and level allocation (each spell has 7 levels), equipment management, and voluntary dungeon depth. Players can choose to retreat. Difficulty emerges from player decisions (alignment restrictions: good/evil cannot party together) and maze design (teleport traps, darkness squares).

progression.upgrade_system: Character leveling system with experience gain from monster kills. Class-based spell progression: each class has multiple spells, each with seven trainable levels. Equipment tiering through dungeon treasure discovery.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Elite classes (Bishop, Samurai, Lord, Ninja) unlock after characters progress sufficiently in levels. Winning party exportable to Wizardry II and III.
replayability_hooks: Five races (Human, Elf, Dwarf, Gnome, Hobbit) × three alignments (Good, Neutral, Evil) × four base classes = 60+ initial party compositions
Good/evil party restrictions create different strategic paths
Permadeath encourages multiple runs with new party configurations
Maze has permanent darkness and teleport trap squares requiring manual charting—each playthrough demands mapmaking
Party export to sequels rewards completion
Elite class unlocks incentivize leveling different class combinations

world_building_techniques: Dungeon-as-narrative: ten progressively deeper levels establish escalating threat (each level more challenging)
Named final boss Werdna (evil arch-wizard) provides story goal without cutscenes
Trebor's castle setting establishes feudal fantasy context
Magic item discovery in dungeon implies lore (amulet as MacGuffin quest object)
Monster encounters and treasure distribution suggest dungeon ecology and history
Text-based town menu (buying supplies, healing, class training) establishes settlement as hub of civilization vs. dungeon as wilderness

boss_design.philosophy: Final confrontation: Werdna, the arch-wizard occupying the bottom dungeon level. Represents culmination of ten-level descent and serves as dungeon's supernatural guardian. Design philosophy emphasizes player preparation (party composition, equipment, spell levels, mana management) over pattern memorization—success depends on party-building choices made across entire run.
notable_bosses: Werdna (final boss, bottom dungeon level, arch-wizard)

faction_indicators: Alignment system (Good, Neutral, Evil) creates factional party identity; good and evil characters cannot party together
Class specialization signals party role and tactical identity (Fighter/Priest/Mage/Thief base classes; Bishop/Samurai/Lord/Ninja elite)
Race selection (Human/Elf/Dwarf/Gnome/Hobbit) adds social identity layer
danger_signals: Permadeath system: losing entire party ends that save file permanently
Teleportation risk: entering wrong coordinates lands party in traps or solid stone, ending game
Darkness squares: navigation hazard in maze, forcing blindness unless player maps carefully
Deep dungeon levels: exponential monster difficulty increase as party descends
Resource depletion: spell uses limited, healing items finite, requiring careful management across ten levels
Total party kill (TPK) unrecoverable unless new party manually rescues corpses from dungeon
environmental_storytelling: Ten-level dungeon maze with increasing monster difficulty tells story of deepening evil
Darkness spell squares and teleportation trap squares suggest magical corruption or wards placed by Werdna
Progressively powerful treasure distribution indicates legendary artifacts stored in dungeon depths
Monster variety across levels implies ecosystem or guardians of increasing power
Final level's arch-wizard Werdna as sanctuary boss signals source of dungeon's corruption
Trebor's castle above dungeon frames it as a sealed threat requiring adventurer intervention

sources_used: wikipedia: Wizardry: Proving Grounds of the Mad Overlord
